description_en: Business signage in Quebec is governed by the Charter of the French Language (as amended by Bill 96), municipal bylaws, and in some cases federal trademark law.

french_language_requirements.administered_by: Office québécois de la langue française (OQLF)

rules.rule_en: French must be markedly predominant — meaning French text must be at least twice the visual size of any other language text.
rules.applies_to: All exterior commercial signs visible to the public

rules.rule_en: Trade names must include a French generic term or description if the name is not already French. A French-language version must be displayed.
French must appear, but other languages can appear alongside French in equal size for interior signage and menus.

municipal_requirements_montreal.authority: Ville de Montréal — Direction de l'urbanisme
municipal_requirements_montreal.when_required_en: Any new exterior sign or change to an existing sign typically requires a municipal sign permit.
restrictions_en: Maximum sign dimensions vary by zoning (commercial, residential, industrial)
Illuminated signs may require additional permits
Heritage buildings have strict signage rules
No roof-top signage in many zones

federal_trademark_note_en: Registered Canadian trademarks may be displayed on signs without French translation, but must still comply with the markedly predominant rule for accompanying descriptive text.
